learningOutcomes: Define chemical reaction and distinguish from physical changes
Identify reactants and products in simple reactions
State the law of conservation of mass
realLifeContexts: Cooking food changes its properties permanently
Burning wood produces ash and smoke
Rusting of iron objects over time
commonMisconceptions: Physical changes and chemical changes are the same
Mass is lost when things burn

learningOutcomes: Write balanced chemical equations for given reactions
Classify reactions as combination, decomposition, displacement, or double displacement
Apply the law of conservation of mass to balance equations
Relate chemical reactions to everyday phenomena
realLifeContexts: Photosynthesis in plants converting CO2 and water to glucose
Respiration in living organisms releasing energy
Combustion of fuels in engines
Corrosion of metals in humid environments
commonMisconceptions: Confusing coefficients with subscripts in chemical formulas
Thinking chemical equations can be balanced by changing subscripts

learningOutcomes: Analyze oxidation-reduction reactions and assign oxidation states
Predict products of reactions using an activity series
Evaluate factors affecting reaction rates
Design experiments to investigate chemical reactions
realLifeContexts: Electrochemical cells and rechargeable batteries
Industrial synthesis of ammonia (Haber process)
Acid rain formation from atmospheric reactions
Biochemical reactions in cellular metabolism
commonMisconceptions: Oxidation always requires oxygen gas
All reactions proceed at the same rate regardless of conditions

learningOutcomes: Define photosynthesis as the process plants use to make food
Identify the inputs (CO2, water, sunlight) and outputs (glucose, oxygen)
State where photosynthesis occurs in plants (chloroplasts)
realLifeContexts: Why plants are green
Why plants need sunlight to grow
Why we depend on plants for the oxygen we breathe
commonMisconceptions: Plants absorb food from the soil like animals eat food
Only leaves are capable of photosynthesis

learningOutcomes: Explain the process of photosynthesis step-by-step
Write the chemical equation for photosynthesis
Describe the role of chlorophyll in capturing light energy
Relate photosynthesis to food chains and energy flow
realLifeContexts: Why forests are called the lungs of the Earth
Impact of deforestation on atmospheric oxygen levels
How aquatic plants contribute to dissolved oxygen in water
Importance of urban trees in reducing CO2 in cities
commonMisconceptions: Plants only perform photosynthesis during the day and stop all processes at night
Oxygen is the primary product of photosynthesis — glucose is

learningOutcomes: Distinguish between light-dependent and light-independent reactions
Analyze factors affecting the rate of photosynthesis (light intensity, CO2 concentration, temperature)
Evaluate the importance of photosynthesis in the global carbon cycle
Design experiments to test variables affecting photosynthesis
realLifeContexts: Optimizing light and CO2 in commercial greenhouse farming
Climate change and declining plant productivity
Biofuel production from algae
Why C4 plants outperform C3 plants in hot, dry climates
commonMisconceptions: Light-independent reactions only occur in darkness
All plant species photosynthesize with equal efficiency

learningOutcomes: Define electric current and voltage in simple terms
Identify components of a simple electric circuit
Distinguish between series and parallel circuits
realLifeContexts: How a flashlight or torch works
Home lighting systems and wall switches
Why one broken bulb in a series string darkens all others
commonMisconceptions: Electric current gets used up as it flows around a circuit
Electricity flows from both battery terminals simultaneously

learningOutcomes: Apply Ohm's Law to calculate voltage, current, or resistance
Calculate total resistance in series and parallel circuits
Explain the relationship between voltage, current, and resistance
Interpret and draw simple circuit diagrams
realLifeContexts: Household electrical wiring using parallel circuits
Why circuit breakers trip during overloading
How mobile phone chargers regulate voltage
Dimmer switches and variable resistance
prerequisites: Understanding of measurement units (volts, amps, ohms)
commonMisconceptions: Voltage and current are the same quantity
All components in a series circuit share the same voltage

learningOutcomes: Analyze complex circuits with multiple resistors using Kirchhoff's laws
Calculate power consumption and energy use in electrical devices
Design circuits for specific current and voltage requirements
Evaluate efficiency and energy loss in real-world circuits
realLifeContexts: Calculating electricity bills using kWh consumption
Designing solar panel charge controller circuits
Battery management systems in electric vehicles
Smart home automation and sensor circuits
commonMisconceptions: Power and energy are interchangeable terms
A battery always provides constant voltage regardless of current drawn

learningOutcomes: Define acids and bases using common examples
Identify acids and bases using litmus paper
Describe the pH scale as a measure of acidity and alkalinity
realLifeContexts: Lemon juice and vinegar taste sour because they are acidic
Soap and baking soda feel slippery because they are basic
Antacid tablets neutralize stomach acid to relieve heartburn
prerequisites: Basic knowledge of matter and substances
commonMisconceptions: All acids are dangerous and corrosive
pH 7 means a substance is harmless

learningOutcomes: Explain the Arrhenius and Brønsted-Lowry definitions of acids and bases
Predict the products of neutralization reactions
Calculate pH from hydrogen ion concentration
Describe the reactions of acids with metals, carbonates, and bases
realLifeContexts: Acid rain damaging building materials and ecosystems
Soil pH affecting agricultural crop yield
Swimming pool maintenance using pH adjustment
Tooth decay from acidic foods and bacteria
commonMisconceptions: Strong acids are always more concentrated than weak acids
Neutralization always produces a pH 7 solution

learningOutcomes: Apply the Lewis acid-base theory to predict reactions
Analyze buffer systems and their role in biological systems
Evaluate acid-base equilibria using Ka and Kb values
Design a titration experiment and interpret results
realLifeContexts: Blood pH regulation by bicarbonate buffer system
Industrial production of sulfuric acid
Water treatment and pH control
Drug efficacy depending on body pH
commonMisconceptions: All acid-base reactions reach completion
A buffer can neutralize unlimited amounts of acid or base

learningOutcomes: Define force as a push or pull that can change an object's motion
Identify contact and non-contact forces with examples
Describe the effect of balanced and unbalanced forces on objects
realLifeContexts: Pushing a shopping trolley to make it move
Friction slowing down a sliding book on a table
Gravity pulling a ball back to the ground after being thrown
prerequisites: Basic understanding of movement and rest
commonMisconceptions: A moving object needs a continuous force to keep moving
Heavier objects always fall faster than lighter ones

learningOutcomes: Apply Newton's three laws of motion to real-world scenarios
Calculate acceleration using F = ma
Analyze the relationship between speed, distance, and time
Distinguish between mass and weight
realLifeContexts: Seatbelts preventing injury due to inertia in car collisions
Rocket propulsion using Newton's Third Law
Athletes using force and timing to maximise performance
Braking distances of vehicles at different speeds
commonMisconceptions: The action and reaction forces in Newton's Third Law cancel each other out
An object with greater mass always has greater weight in all situations

learningOutcomes: Analyze motion using velocity-time and displacement-time graphs
Apply the equations of motion to solve multi-step problems
Evaluate the role of friction, air resistance, and tension in real systems
Calculate momentum and apply the law of conservation of momentum
realLifeContexts: Crumple zones in cars absorbing impact forces over longer time
Satellites maintaining orbit through centripetal force
Athletes improving performance through projectile optimization
Airbag deployment timing and impulse
commonMisconceptions: Centripetal force is a separate force added to a system
Momentum and kinetic energy are always conserved together

learningOutcomes: State that all living things are made of cells
Identify the basic parts of a cell: cell membrane, cytoplasm, nucleus
Distinguish between plant and animal cells
realLifeContexts: Why plants are rigid — they have a cell wall
Why leaves are green — chloroplasts contain chlorophyll
How cuts heal — cells divide to replace damaged ones
commonMisconceptions: All cells look the same regardless of their function
The cell wall and cell membrane are the same structure

learningOutcomes: Describe the structure and function of key organelles
Explain how specialised cells are adapted to their function
Compare prokaryotic and eukaryotic cells
Explain the process of cell division (mitosis) in outline
realLifeContexts: Red blood cells lacking a nucleus to carry more haemoglobin
Muscle cells packed with mitochondria for energy production
Root hair cells with large surface area for water absorption
Cancer as uncontrolled cell division
commonMisconceptions: Only animal cells undergo cell division
Mitochondria are only found in animal cells

learningOutcomes: Analyze the fluid mosaic model of the cell membrane
Evaluate the role of osmosis and active transport in cells
Explain meiosis and its role in genetic variation
Assess how cell cycle regulation failures lead to cancer
realLifeContexts: Why red blood cells crenate in salt water and burst in fresh water
How dialysis machines mimic kidney cell membrane function
IVF technology and embryo cell division
Targeted cancer therapies that disrupt cell cycle checkpoints
commonMisconceptions: Osmosis requires a pump or energy input
Meiosis and mitosis produce identical outcomes

learningOutcomes: Describe the periodic table as an organised arrangement of elements
Identify periods (rows) and groups (columns) in the periodic table
State the properties of metals and non-metals from their position
realLifeContexts: Aluminium foil and copper wire are metals from the periodic table
Oxygen and nitrogen are non-metals we breathe every day
Noble gases like helium are used in balloons and lighting
prerequisites: Basic knowledge of elements and atoms
commonMisconceptions: The periodic table is just a list of elements in alphabetical order
All metals are solid at room temperature

learningOutcomes: Explain trends in atomic radius, ionization energy, and electronegativity across periods and down groups
Predict the reactivity of elements based on their group
Describe the properties of alkali metals, halogens, and noble gases
Explain how electron configuration determines an element's group
realLifeContexts: Why alkali metals like sodium react violently with water
Why halogens are used as disinfectants in swimming pools
Why noble gases are used in inert atmospheres for food packaging
Trends exploited in designing new battery materials
commonMisconceptions: Elements in the same group always have the same number of electrons
Ionization energy always decreases as you go down a group

learningOutcomes: Apply quantum mechanical model to explain orbital filling and periodic trends
Analyze anomalies in periodic trends and explain their causes
Predict chemical behaviour of unfamiliar elements from their position
Evaluate the historical development of the periodic table
realLifeContexts: Lanthanides in modern electronics and MRI machines
Transition metals as industrial catalysts
Superheavy element synthesis at research facilities
Predictive chemistry: Mendeleev predicting properties of undiscovered elements
commonMisconceptions: Electron orbitals are fixed circular paths around the nucleus
All anomalies in periodic trends are errors in the data

learningOutcomes: Define energy and state that it cannot be created or destroyed
Identify common forms of energy: kinetic, potential, thermal, light, sound
Give examples of energy transformations in everyday life
realLifeContexts: A roller coaster converting potential energy to kinetic energy
A light bulb converting electrical energy to light and heat
Food providing chemical energy for the body to move
prerequisites: Basic understanding of forces and motion
commonMisconceptions: Energy is used up or destroyed when it is converted
Potential energy only refers to objects held up high

learningOutcomes: Calculate kinetic and gravitational potential energy using formulas
Apply the law of conservation of energy to solve problems
Explain what efficiency means and calculate it for energy transfers
Describe renewable and non-renewable energy sources and their trade-offs
realLifeContexts: Hydroelectric dams converting gravitational potential energy to electricity
Why LED bulbs are more efficient than incandescent bulbs
Solar panels converting light energy to electrical energy
Fuel efficiency ratings in vehicles
commonMisconceptions: A 100% efficient machine is physically achievable
Renewable energy sources have no environmental impact

learningOutcomes: Apply the first and second laws of thermodynamics to real systems
Analyze energy losses in heat engines using Sankey diagrams
Evaluate the role of entropy in energy transformations
Assess the global energy challenge and emerging solutions
realLifeContexts: Why no real engine achieves Carnot efficiency
Thermal power station energy flow analysis
Hydrogen fuel cells as future energy carriers
Energy storage challenges for renewable grids
commonMisconceptions: The second law of thermodynamics can be violated with clever engineering
Entropy is simply disorder with no quantitative meaning

learningOutcomes: Define an ecosystem as a community of living organisms interacting with their environment
Identify producers, consumers, and decomposers in an ecosystem
Construct a simple food chain from given organisms
realLifeContexts: A garden with plants, insects, birds, and soil bacteria
Why removing a predator like wolves changes the whole landscape
How dead leaves and animals are broken down by fungi and bacteria
commonMisconceptions: Decomposers are not important members of ecosystems
Food chains are always simple straight lines in nature

learningOutcomes: Explain energy flow through trophic levels using the 10% rule
Analyze a food web and predict the impact of removing a species
Describe how nutrients cycle through ecosystems (carbon and water cycles)
Distinguish between biodiversity and population size
realLifeContexts: Why there are far fewer top predators than herbivores in any ecosystem
How deforestation affects local rainfall patterns through the water cycle
Coral bleaching as a cascade effect from ocean warming
Reintroduction of wolves in Yellowstone and ecosystem recovery
commonMisconceptions: Removing one species from a food web has no effect on others
Humans are outside of and unaffected by natural ecosystems

learningOutcomes: Evaluate the role of biogeochemical cycles in sustaining life on Earth
Analyze the impact of human activities on ecosystem stability
Apply ecological models to predict population dynamics
Assess conservation strategies using ecological principles
realLifeContexts: Fertiliser runoff causing algal blooms and dead zones in oceans
Climate change shifting biome boundaries poleward
Invasive species like cane toads disrupting native ecosystems
Marine protected areas and their measurable ecological recovery
commonMisconceptions: Ecosystems naturally recover from any disturbance given enough time
Conservation only matters for rare or endangered species

learningOutcomes: Identify and describe the three main states of matter: solid, liquid, gas
Explain how heating and cooling cause changes of state
Use particle theory to describe the arrangement and movement of particles in each state
realLifeContexts: Ice melting into water and water evaporating into steam
Dew forming on glass in the morning as condensation
Why a gas fills the entire container it is placed in
prerequisites: Basic concept of matter and materials
commonMisconceptions: There is no space between particles in a solid
Evaporation and boiling are the same process

learningOutcomes: Explain changes of state in terms of energy and interparticle forces
Interpret heating and cooling curves, identifying plateaus
Distinguish between evaporation and boiling
Describe diffusion and explain factors that affect its rate
realLifeContexts: Why sweating cools the body through evaporative cooling
Dry ice (solid CO2) sublimating without passing through liquid state
Perfume diffusing across a room from a single source
Pressure cookers raising the boiling point of water
commonMisconceptions: Temperature increases during a change of state
Gases have no mass because they cannot be seen

learningOutcomes: Apply the ideal gas laws (Boyle's, Charles's, Gay-Lussac's) to solve problems
Use the kinetic molecular theory to explain gas behaviour
Interpret phase diagrams to predict states at given conditions
Evaluate real gas deviations from ideal behaviour
realLifeContexts: Why aerosol cans warn against high temperatures (pressure increase)
How scuba diving tanks store compressed gas safely
Liquefied natural gas (LNG) transportation at low temperature
Supercritical CO2 as a green solvent in decaffeinating coffee
commonMisconceptions: The ideal gas law applies perfectly to all real gases
Absolute zero means all molecular motion ceases entirely
